mnemonics.title: Remembering Condition Flags
mnemonics.content: N = Negative (MSB = 1), Z = Zero (result = 0), C = Carry (unsigned overflow), V = oVerflow (signed overflow). Think: 'Not Zero Carries oVerflow' or N-Z-C-V in alphabetical order.

mnemonics.title: CPSR Bit Positions
mnemonics.content: Bits 31-28: N, Z, C, V flags; Bit 27: Q (saturation); Bit 24: J (Java state); Bit 5: T (Thumb); Bits 7-6: I, F (interrupt disables); Bits 4-0: M (mode bits). Remember: 'Flags Quick TJ I Found Missing' or 'N Z C V Q J IT M'.

mnemonics.title: Exception Mode Saves
mnemonics.content: When exception occurs: CPSR → SPSR_mode, PC → LR_mode, Mode switches to exception mode, I and F bits set. Remember: 'SPL' - Save to SPSR, Push to Link register, Load new mode.

acronyms.usage: Stores processor state, flags, and control information during program execution
Temporarily stores CPSR during exception handling for later restoration
C indicates unsigned arithmetic overflow; V indicates signed arithmetic overflow
I disables IRQ, F disables FIQ when set to 1
IRQ is standard priority interrupt; FIQ is high-priority fast interrupt
T=0: ARM mode (32-bit instructions); T=1: Thumb mode (16-bit compressed instructions)

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 32-bit register with labeled sections: Bits 31-28 (N,Z,C,V), Bit 27 (Q), Bit 24 (J), Bit 5 (T), Bits 7-6 (I,F), Bits 4-0 (Mode). Use a horizontal bar divided into colored sections.
Show a simple flowchart: Operation → Calculate result → Check each condition → Set/clear flags accordingly. Color-code each flag with conditions.
Draw a sequence: Normal execution → Exception occurs → Save CPSR to SPSR → Save PC to LR → Change mode → Disable interrupts → Jump to exception vector.

analogies.analogy: Think of CPSR like a car dashboard. The condition flags (N,Z,C,V) are like warning lights showing the status of the last operation (like fuel level). The control bits (I,F,T,M) are like the gear selector, ignition, and mode buttons controlling how the car (processor) operates.
SPSR is like a spare tire - you only use it when something goes wrong (exception). You store your current state (CPSR) in it so you can return to normal operation after fixing the problem.

Changing processor modes is like switching roles at work. When an interrupt (phone call) comes, you save your current task, switch to 'meeting mode' (exception mode), handle it, then return to your previous task.
